A cephalometric study in people demonstrated the thickness of the hard tissue barrier between primary incisor teeth and their successors was <3 mm ( 10 ). However, no such data regarding the barrier between the deciduous mandibular canine teeth and the developing tooth buds exists for dogs which may have assisted in avoiding the potential disruptive effect of extractions on permanent tooth buds during odontogenesis. Deciduous canine teeth in dogs are long, thin, and fragile and operator technique during extraction may significantly traumatize the developing permanent tooth. Gentle tissue handling, and good hand control are essential while extracting the deciduous teeth. In addition, if a deciduous canine tooth fractures during extraction, removal of the root tip is crucial, because retained root tips may alter the eruption of the permanent tooth, and may lead to infection, further potentiating the formation of enamel defects. Prior to extracting a deciduous tooth, obtaining preoperative dental radiographs is essential to determine the shape and location of the root and to document the presence and location of the developing permanent tooth ( 11 ). Because of the close three-dimensional spatial anatomic relationship between the apex of primary teeth and the developing permanent tooth the use of a cone beam CT (CBCT) scan is beneficial in assessing more precisely the exact spatial relationship of the crown and the apex in relation to the permanent successor ( 12 ). Regardless of the extraction technique employed, post-extraction dental radiographs should be obtained to document complete removal of the deciduous canine and to potentially identify damage to the developing tooth bud. Figure 4 Dental radiograph of the left mandibular deciduous canine tooth (white arrow) and the developing permanent canine tooth (white arrowhead). Note the proximity of the apex of a deciduous mandibular canine to the crown of a developing permanent canine tooth. The incidence and rate of enamel defects on permanent dentition following extraction of deciduous teeth in dogs has not been reported. Hence, the primary objective of this retrospective study was to determine an incident rate of enamel defects occurring following extraction of linguoverted mandibular deciduous canine teeth in young dogs which were causing palatal trauma, dental interlock, and signs of oral pain.

[methods] Methods and materials Medical records from five veterinary specialty dentistry and oral surgery practices were reviewed for dogs who underwent deciduous mandibular canine teeth extraction due to linguoverted canine teeth between January 2017 and December 2021. Dogs from 38 breeds included in the study were between 8 and 12 weeks of age at the time of surgery and had class 1 or class 2 malocclusions with linguoverted mandibular canine teeth. All dogs underwent surgical extractions of deciduous mandibular canine teeth (i.e., an open extraction technique) and returned for at least one in-person recheck exam to evaluate the permanent mandibular canine teeth. Dogs were excluded from the study if they were older than 12 weeks of age at the time of extraction, had deciduous incisor teeth extracted at the same time as the deciduous canine teeth, did not undergo an in-person recheck exam, or were lost to follow up. Patient information collected during original presentation included patient history, signalment, physical exam findings, oral exam findings including descriptions and photographs of the malocclusion. Information collected during recheck exams included written descriptions and photographs of enamel defects in permanent mandibular canine teeth and whether patients required additional treatment to prevent the permanent canine teeth from causing palatal and/or gingival trauma. Surgical teeth extraction was performed as previously described ( 13 ). Briefly, a sulcular incision was made followed by raising a triangle mucosal flap using a periosteal elevator. Partial buccal alveolectomy was performed using a round burr on a high-speed handpiece followed by luxating the tooth using appropriate size luxator. Delivery of the tooth was done using extraction forceps ( 13 ). All surgical incisions were closed using an absorbable monofilament suture material in a simple interrupted fashion. Pre and post extraction radiographs were obtained for all patients included in this study. Statistical analysis Statistical analyses were performed using SAS 9.4 (Cary, NC). A significance threshold of p < 0.05 was used. The assumption of normality for age was evaluated via inspection of QQ- and PP-plots, histograms, and skewness. Age was determined to not be normally distributed variables were summarized descriptively with median and interquartile range (IQR). Frequencies were reported as both numbers and percentages with 95% CIs where appropriate. Univariable logistic regression analyses were used to test for association of patient sex and age with odds of having an enamel defect. Log-likelihood p -values and odds ratios with profile-likelihood odds ratio confidence limits were reported.

[results] Results The search of medical records from five veterinary dental specialty practices for dogs between the age of 8–12 weeks that underwent surgical extraction of deciduous mandibular canine teeth yielded 128 cases. Fifty-four cases were not reexamined following extraction and were not included in the study. Therefore, the final group of patients included in the study was 74 individual dogs where extraction of mandibular deciduous canine teeth was performed due to linguoverted mandibular canine teeth. The 74 dogs included in the study represented 38 breeds with standard poodles (9.5%, 7/74), poodle and poodle crosses (including ‘doodle’ breeds) (21.6%, 6/74), and Labradors (6.7%, 5/74) being the most represented. Standard poodles, poodles, and poodle crosses were overrepresented accounting for 31% of the dogs included in the study. At the time of surgery, all dogs were sexually intact. There were 35 females and 39 males with a median (IQR) age of 12 ( 10–12 ) weeks at the time of tooth extractions for dogs that eventually did not exhibit enamel defects on permanent canine teeth and 10 ( 10 , 11 ) weeks for dogs that did not exhibit enamel defects on permanent canine teeth following extraction. The age per week odds ratio (95%CI) was 0.72 (95%CI 0.48–1.07) ( p = 0.098). Hence, based on the 95% confidence interval the odds of an enamel defect could decrease by as much 52% per week older an animal is or increase by as much as 7% per week older an animal is. This broad range demonstrates that while the dog’s age at the time of surgery was not significantly associated with the development of enamel defects, a clinically important association cannot be ruled out. We noted that 10.4% (5/48) of dogs aged 11–12 weeks at the time of extraction and 30.7% (8/26) of dogs aged 8–10 weeks at the time of extraction subsequently developed enamel defects on the permanent canine teeth. The enamel defect rate was 20% (7/35) in female dogs and 15% (6/39) in male dogs [ p = 0.603, female vs. male OR (95%CI) = 1.4 (0.4–4.7)]. There was no significant association between sex and odds of an enamel defect. In total 74 dogs had 143 teeth extracted, with 69 dogs having bilateral extraction of mandibular deciduous canines and 5 having unilateral extraction. Of the 74 dogs who underwent surgical extraction, 13/74 had enamel defects on adult mandibular canine teeth 17.5% (95%CI 11–28%). In total there were 21 enamel defects noted on the permanent mandibular canine teeth of these 13 dogs which is an enamel defect rate of 14.6% (21/143). In addition, 37.8% (28/74) of dogs required additional treatment to prevent the permanent mandibular canine teeth from traumatizing the hard palate. These treatments included crown reduction and vital pulp therapy (82%, 23/28) and wedge gingivectomy (17.8%, 5/28).

[discussion] Discussion Dogs exhibiting linguoversion of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth typically requires prompt treatment to alleviate pain, soft tissue trauma to the hard palate or gingiva, and to prevent or release any dental interlock if present ( 14 ). This practice is known as interceptive orthodontics and is often performed in young dogs. This is the first study to document the occurrence and rate of enamel defects that occurs due to damage to the developing teeth buds likely during extractions of deciduous canine teeth. We noted several clinically relevant findings. First, 17.5% of dogs had enamel defects on permanent mandibular canine teeth. Specifically, an incidence rate of 14.6% of permanent canine teeth exhibited enamel defects. In addition, the dog’s age at the time of extraction was not significantly correlated with the development of enamel defects. Finally, 37.8% of dogs required additional treatment to prevent the permanent mandibular canine teeth from traumatizing the hard palate. Enamel defects may be genetic or environmental and often the exact etiology is unknown. Environmental or acquired enamel defects may be divided into those caused by local factors such as trauma, local inflammation or infection, and those caused by systemic factors such as prolonged fever, systemic infection such as distemper virus ( 15 ), excessive fluoride administration, and certain drugs. Specifically for the present study, a local factor was suspected when a single tooth or group of neighboring teeth were affected resulting in a Turner’s tooth or Turner’s hypoplasia. Unlike local occurrence, general systemic factors during development of the teeth may result in several or all the teeth being affected (i.e., semigeneralized or generalized occurrence). There are numerous hereditary, acquired, systemic and local etiological factors which are associated with enamel defects ( 16 ). Because enamel does not remodel, the defects theoretically present a record of the insults suffered by the enamel organ during development of the enamel. However, determining the specific timing of insults to the developing enamel is often difficult due to the current lack of knowledge regarding the chronology of the different stages of amelogenesis as well as individual variation in rates of enamel formation ( 16 ). The damage sustained by the permanent canine teeth in this study likely occurred during amelogenesis or the process of enamel formation during odontogenesis. Ameloblasts are the cells that produce enamel ( 17 ). Their life cycle is divided into six stages that include morphogenetic, organizing, formative, maturative, protective, and desmolytic. Enamel matrix is secreted in the formative stage whereas mineralization of the enamel matrix occurs in the maturation stage ( 18 ). During enamel maturation, a dynamic process with cellular, biochemical, genetic, and epigenetic changes takes place in the developing tissue ( 18 ). The developing dental enamel is highly susceptible to different systemic and local factors during the formative and maturative stages of amelogenesis. Due to the close anatomical proximity of the apex of the deciduous canine teeth to the developing permanent tooth bud, extraction of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth may damage the developing permanent teeth ( 19 ). Damage resulting in a Turner’s tooth or Turner’s hypoplasia may occur acutely because of trauma by direct impact of the root of the deciduous tooth on the permanent tooth germ or by mechanical trauma related to instrumentation and improper extraction technique or because of inflammation or infection during the post-operative period ( 20 ). Although all the extractions in this study were performed with an open extraction technique, a closed extraction technique may be utilized when appropriate. Specifically, deciduous teeth that have undergone a substantial amount of root resorption as indicated on a pre-operative radiograph and may already be mobile may be amendable for closed extraction technique (i.e., non-surgical) ( 21 ). To the authors knowledge, it has not been reported if extraction technique influences the rate of enamel defects following extraction. Inflammation and infection may play a role in the development of enamel defects post-operatively, there is no study in dogs linking extraction of deciduous teeth and the development of enamel defects due to post-operative inflammation or infection. One study in people found that if caries occurs in a primary tooth, the successor tooth is more than twice as likely to have an enamel defect and in the case of early tooth loss for reasons other than trauma such as extraction or infection, the permanent successor tooth was five times more likely to have an enamel defect ( 22 ). In the present study the role of post-operative inflammation vs. trauma to the developing permanent tooth germ during extraction leading to an enamel defect is unknown. There was no apparent post-operative infection and none of the dogs were treated with antibiotics. Hence, infection may not have been a contributing factor to the development of enamel defects in this study, however, this cannot be asserted. We noted the dog’s age at the time of extraction was not significantly correlated with the development of enamel defects. The current published recommendation is to extract linguoverted deciduous mandibular canine teeth as soon as they are observed, which may be as early as 6–8 weeks of age ( 23 ). This recommendation was made to eliminate trauma to the soft tissues of the hard palate and gingiva, release dental interlock which may allow the mandibles to reach their full genetic potential, and to alleviate pain. Although age at the time of extractions and the development of enamel defects was not significant, the fact that 10.4% of dogs age11-12 weeks at the time of extraction and 30.7% of dogs age 8–10 weeks at the time of extraction subsequently developed enamel defects may suggest to wait on extracting deciduous mandibular canine teeth until the dog is older than 11 weeks to minimize the potential for damaging the developing permanent tooth bud. Importantly, it is unknown if a delay in performing extraction of mandibular deciduous canine teeth at 8–10 weeks of age until 11–12 weeks of age or older will affect the ability of the jaws to reach full genetic growth potential due to continued dental interlock and potential interference with skull development. A delay in extractions also has the potential for ongoing palatal trauma and patient discomfort. Timing of extractions is an area where further research is needed to provide a recommendation for the optimal time to perform extractions of deciduous canine teeth and minimize the occurrence of enamel defects in the permanent canine teeth as well as prevent dental interlock which may affect the ability of the jaws to reach their full genetic potential in both length and width ( 24 ). Ultimately, when indicated, the extraction of deciduous teeth should be performed with great care and requires both proper extraction technique and carful operator technique. Pre- and post-extraction dental radiographs are essential when planning extractions to both identify the shape and location of the root and document the presence and location of the developing permanent tooth, as well as any damage present to the developing permanent tooth. The potential for damage to the tooth bud of developing permanent teeth should be discussed with the client prior to interceptive orthodontics ( 25 ). This should include the risks of negative sequelae associated with enamel defects such as increased plaque retention, weakened tooth structure, pulpitis, dentinal sensitivity, and endodontic disease. Finally, it should be noted that 37.8% of dogs required additional treatment to prevent the permanent mandibular canine teeth from traumatizing the hard palate. The possibility of additional treatment once the permanent canine teeth erupt should also be clearly communicated to the client. Due to the retrospective nature of this study, evaluations of the 54 patients lost to follow up was not possible. It is possible that patients who were lost to follow up were perceived to be successful and/or functional in nature by their owners and primary care veterinarians and did not warrant further orthodontic or restorative intervention. This may have potentially altered the rate of enamel defects noted following extraction of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth.

[conclusion] Conclusion Extraction of linguoverted deciduous mandibular canine teeth is performed as an “interceptive” orthodontic procedure. However, we noted that those teeth extractions may result in enamel defects on the permanent teeth in 17.5% of dogs and an enamel defect rate of 14.6%. Informing clients that the potential for damage to the developing tooth bud may result in enamel defects on the permanent mandibular canine teeth is warranted.

retrieved_papers.title: Cone-beam computed tomography for assessment of palatal displaced canine position: a methodological study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2014]
[Abstract] To assess the inter- and intraexaminer reliability of a measurement method for evaluation of eruption angles and position of palatal displaced canines (PDCs) with cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) images and to test the validity of the measured angles on a dry skull.
Twenty patients (eight boys, 12 girls; age 11.4±1.2 years) were randomly chosen among 67 patients from a study evaluating the interceptive effect of extracting the deciduous canine in children with PDCs. In total, 60 images were analyzed, because each patient had three CBCT examinations (baseline, 6-month control, and endpoint). Two observers assessed the following measurements twice: mesioangular and sagittal angle, vertical position, canine cusp tip, and canine apex to dental arch. The validity of the angular measurements was tested against angular measurements on a dry skull using mathematical formulations.
The inter- and intraexaminer mean differences for angular and linear measurements were all low and statistically insignificant (P>.05). The mean differences between the physical and 3D measurements were 0.5±0.39 mm for the sagittal angle and 0.22±0.19 mm for the mesioangular angle.
Linear and angular measurements on CBCT images are accurate and precise and can be used to assess the precise position of a PDC.

[methods] Materials and Methods: Twenty patients (eight boys, 12 girls; age 11.4 ± 1.2 years) were randomly chosen among 67 patients from a study evaluating the interceptive effect of extracting the deciduous canine in children with PDCs. In total, 60 images were analyzed, because each patient had three CBCT examinations (baseline, 6-month control, and endpoint). Two observers assessed the following measurements twice: mesioangular and sagittal angle, vertical position, canine cusp tip, and canine apex to dental arch. The validity of the angular measurements was tested against angular measurements on a dry skull using mathematical formulations.

[results] Results: The inter- and intraexaminer mean differences for angular and linear measurements were all low and statistically insignificant ( P > .05). The mean differences between the physical and 3D measurements were 0.5 ± 0.39 mm for the sagittal angle and 0.22 ± 0.19 mm for the mesioangular angle.

[conclusion] Conclusions: Linear and angular measurements on CBCT images are accurate and precise and can be used to assess the precise position of a PDC.

retrieved_papers.title: Incidence of enamel defects on permanent canine teeth following extraction of linguoverted mandibular deciduous canine teeth in dogs.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2024]
[Abstract] Interceptive orthodontics may be indicated in puppies exhibiting dental malocclusion with linguoverted deciduous mandibular canine teeth to alleviate pain and prevent teeth interlock, which may affect growth and development of the mandibles. Historically extraction of deciduous mandibular canine teeth has been recommended as soon as a malocclusion is identified, often as early as 6-8 weeks of age and no later than 12 weeks of age. This early surgical intervention of deciduous teeth extractions risks potential damage to the developing permanent canine teeth resulting in enamel defects often referred to as a Turner's tooth or Turner's hypoplasia. A search of medical records from five veterinary specialty dentistry practices was conducted to identify dogs 8-12 weeks of age who (a) underwent deciduous mandibular canine extractions for management of class 1 or class 2 malocclusion with linguoverted mandibular canine teeth, and (b) were seen for at least one recheck exam to assess for enamel defects on permanent mandibular canine teeth. Furthermore, data was collected to determine the number of dogs that required additional treatment after eruption of the permanent canine teeth due to linguoversion of the permanent canine teeth. All procedures were performed by a board-certified veterinary dentist™ or a supervised veterinary dentistry resident. Seventy-four dogs fit the inclusion criteria and had a total of 143 deciduous mandibular canine teeth extracted, out of which 13 dogs exhibited enamel defects affecting 21 permanent canine teeth. The 13 affected dogs represent a 17.5% cumulative incident rate 13/74 (95%CI 11-28%). Of all extracted teeth, 14.6% (21/143) had enamel defects affecting permanent canine teeth. Twenty-eight dogs required additional treatment to prevent the permanent mandibular canine teeth from causing trauma to the hard palate and gingiva which represented 37.8% (28/74) of all dogs in the study. Age and sex of the dog at the time of extraction were not found to be associated with the likelihood of incidence of enamel defects. This is the first reported rate of enamel defects on permanent mandibular canine teeth following extraction of deciduous mandibular canine teeth and is important to consider when advising or performing extraction of deciduous teeth in dogs.

[Introduction] Introduction Interceptive orthodontics is a proactive orthodontic treatment that is presumed to allow for the development of normal permanent occlusion in puppies exhibiting malocclusion ( 1 , 2 ). For this study classification of malocclusions were made according to American Veterinary Dental College (AVDC) Nomenclature. The AVDC defines a class 1 malocclusion as neutroclusion with normal rostrocaudal relationship of the maxillary and mandibular dental arches and malposition of one or more individual teeth and class 2 malocclusion as mandibular distoclusion with an abnormal rostrocaudal relationship between the dental arches in which the mandibular arch occludes caudal to its normal position relative to the maxillary arch ( 3 ). In this study interceptive orthodontics involved extraction of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth in patients with a class 1 or class 2 malocclusion and linguoverted canine teeth ( Figure 1 ). Extraction of deciduous mandibular canine teeth should be performed with an abundance of caution to prevent damage to the developing permanent canine tooth. Given that enamel development ceases prior to or at the time of tooth eruption, enamel in dogs should be fully developed in permanent teeth 15–31.4 weeks after birth ( 4 ). This period of development makes enamel susceptible to injury from a variety of causes both environmental and systemic. Figure 1 A class 2 malocclusion and linguoversion of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth (white arrow) in an 11-week-old dog resulting in trauma to the hard palate. Most enamel defects are typically caused by trauma, infection, or inflammation resulting in a disruption during tooth development ( 4 ). Traumatic damage to the dental organ during development may result in enamel hypoplasia or hypocalcification, characterized by defects that are localized, irregular, and often affecting a single tooth ( 5 ). When enamel hypoplasia or enamel defects affect only one tooth they are known as a Turner’s tooth or exhibiting Turner’s hypoplasia ( 6 ). Likewise, systemic processes, such as fever, distemper, hypocalcemia, nutritional deficiencies, excessive intake of fluoride, and some drugs taken during the period of enamel formation, may result in enamel dysplasia. In these cases, all teeth developing at that time are affected. Hypoplasia is defined as a quantitative defect of enamel visually and is histomorphologically identified as an external defect involving the surface of the enamel and associated with reduced thickness of enamel ( 7 , 8 ). The defective enamel may occur as shallow or deep pits or rows of pits arranged horizontally, or as small or large, wide or narrow grooves. Silberman et al. ( 9 ) created a simplified hypoplasia index ( Table 1 ) which was used to characterize enamel hypoplasia of the affected teeth in this study. In the present study, all dogs had type-IV enamel hypoplasia due to trauma during extraction or post-operative inflammation resulting in brown and yellow enamel discoloration, abnormal coalescence, and missing enamel with the majority having defects on the lateral or occlusal aspect of the permanent canine teeth ( Figures 2 , 3 ). Table 1 Simplified tooth hypoplasia index ( 9 ). Type I hypoplasia: enamel discoloration due to hypoplasia Type II hypoplasia: abnormal coalescence due to hypoplasia Type III hypoplasia: missing some parts of enamel due to hypoplasia Type IV hypoplasia: a combination of previous three types of hypoplasia Figure 2 Enamel defects on a permanent right mandibular canine tooth and the right mandibular 3rd incisor (Black arrows). Figure 3 Enamel defect on a permanent right mandibular canine tooth (white arrow). The close anatomical relationship of the apex of the deciduous tooth to the developing permanent tooth bud explains the potential for possible developmental disturbances during extraction ( Figure 4 ). A cephalometric study in people demonstrated the thickness of the hard tissue barrier between primary incisor teeth and their successors was <3 mm ( 10 ). However, no such data regarding the barrier between the deciduous mandibular canine teeth and the developing tooth buds exists for dogs which may have assisted in avoiding the potential disruptive effect of extractions on permanent tooth buds during odontogenesis. Deciduous canine teeth in dogs are long, thin, and fragile and operator technique during extraction may significantly traumatize the developing permanent tooth. Gentle tissue handling, and good hand control are essential while extracting the deciduous teeth. In addition, if a deciduous canine tooth fractures during extraction, removal of the root tip is crucial, because retained root tips may alter the eruption of the permanent tooth, and may lead to infection, further potentiating the formation of enamel defects. Prior to extracting a deciduous tooth, obtaining preoperative dental radiographs is essential to determine the shape and location of the root and to document the presence and location of the developing permanent tooth ( 11 ). Because of the close three-dimensional spatial anatomic relationship between the apex of primary teeth and the developing permanent tooth the use of a cone beam CT (CBCT) scan is beneficial in assessing more precisely the exact spatial relationship of the crown and the apex in relation to the permanent successor ( 12 ). Regardless of the extraction technique employed, post-extraction dental radiographs should be obtained to document complete removal of the deciduous canine and to potentially identify damage to the developing tooth bud. Figure 4 Dental radiograph of the left mandibular deciduous canine tooth (white arrow) and the developing permanent canine tooth (white arrowhead). Note the proximity of the apex of a deciduous mandibular canine to the crown of a developing permanent canine tooth. The incidence and rate of enamel defects on permanent dentition following extraction of deciduous teeth in dogs has not been reported. Hence, the primary objective of this retrospective study was to determine an incident rate of enamel defects occurring following extraction of linguoverted mandibular deciduous canine teeth in young dogs which were causing palatal trauma, dental interlock, and signs of oral pain.

This broad range demonstrates that while the dog’s age at the time of surgery was not significantly associated with the development of enamel defects, a clinically important association cannot be ruled out. We noted that 10.4% (5/48) of dogs aged 11–12 weeks at the time of extraction and 30.7% (8/26) of dogs aged 8–10 weeks at the time of extraction subsequently developed enamel defects on the permanent canine teeth. The enamel defect rate was 20% (7/35) in female dogs and 15% (6/39) in male dogs [ p = 0.603, female vs. male OR (95%CI) = 1.4 (0.4–4.7)]. There was no significant association between sex and odds of an enamel defect. In total 74 dogs had 143 teeth extracted, with 69 dogs having bilateral extraction of mandibular deciduous canines and 5 having unilateral extraction. Of the 74 dogs who underwent surgical extraction, 13/74 had enamel defects on adult mandibular canine teeth 17.5% (95%CI 11–28%). In total there were 21 enamel defects noted on the permanent mandibular canine teeth of these 13 dogs which is an enamel defect rate of 14.6% (21/143). In addition, 37.8% (28/74) of dogs required additional treatment to prevent the permanent mandibular canine teeth from traumatizing the hard palate. These treatments included crown reduction and vital pulp therapy (82%, 23/28) and wedge gingivectomy (17.8%, 5/28).

[discussion] Discussion Dogs exhibiting linguoversion of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth typically requires prompt treatment to alleviate pain, soft tissue trauma to the hard palate or gingiva, and to prevent or release any dental interlock if present ( 14 ). This practice is known as interceptive orthodontics and is often performed in young dogs. This is the first study to document the occurrence and rate of enamel defects that occurs due to damage to the developing teeth buds likely during extractions of deciduous canine teeth. We noted several clinically relevant findings. First, 17.5% of dogs had enamel defects on permanent mandibular canine teeth. Specifically, an incidence rate of 14.6% of permanent canine teeth exhibited enamel defects. In addition, the dog’s age at the time of extraction was not significantly correlated with the development of enamel defects. Finally, 37.8% of dogs required additional treatment to prevent the permanent mandibular canine teeth from traumatizing the hard palate. Enamel defects may be genetic or environmental and often the exact etiology is unknown. Environmental or acquired enamel defects may be divided into those caused by local factors such as trauma, local inflammation or infection, and those caused by systemic factors such as prolonged fever, systemic infection such as distemper virus ( 15 ), excessive fluoride administration, and certain drugs. Specifically for the present study, a local factor was suspected when a single tooth or group of neighboring teeth were affected resulting in a Turner’s tooth or Turner’s hypoplasia. Unlike local occurrence, general systemic factors during development of the teeth may result in several or all the teeth being affected (i.e., semigeneralized or generalized occurrence). There are numerous hereditary, acquired, systemic and local etiological factors which are associated with enamel defects ( 16 ). Because enamel does not remodel, the defects theoretically present a record of the insults suffered by the enamel organ during development of the enamel. However, determining the specific timing of insults to the developing enamel is often difficult due to the current lack of knowledge regarding the chronology of the different stages of amelogenesis as well as individual variation in rates of enamel formation ( 16 ). The damage sustained by the permanent canine teeth in this study likely occurred during amelogenesis or the process of enamel formation during odontogenesis. Ameloblasts are the cells that produce enamel ( 17 ). Their life cycle is divided into six stages that include morphogenetic, organizing, formative, maturative, protective, and desmolytic. Enamel matrix is secreted in the formative stage whereas mineralization of the enamel matrix occurs in the maturation stage ( 18 ). During enamel maturation, a dynamic process with cellular, biochemical, genetic, and epigenetic changes takes place in the developing tissue ( 18 ). The developing dental enamel is highly susceptible to different systemic and local factors during the formative and maturative stages of amelogenesis. Due to the close anatomical proximity of the apex of the deciduous canine teeth to the developing permanent tooth bud, extraction of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth may damage the developing permanent teeth ( 19 ). Damage resulting in a Turner’s tooth or Turner’s hypoplasia may occur acutely because of trauma by direct impact of the root of the deciduous tooth on the permanent tooth germ or by mechanical trauma related to instrumentation and improper extraction technique or because of inflammation or infection during the post-operative period ( 20 ). Although all the extractions in this study were performed with an open extraction technique, a closed extraction technique may be utilized when appropriate. Specifically, deciduous teeth that have undergone a substantial amount of root resorption as indicated on a pre-operative radiograph and may already be mobile may be amendable for closed extraction technique (i.e., non-surgical) ( 21 ). To the authors knowledge, it has not been reported if extraction technique influences the rate of enamel defects following extraction. Inflammation and infection may play a role in the development of enamel defects post-operatively, there is no study in dogs linking extraction of deciduous teeth and the development of enamel defects due to post-operative inflammation or infection. One study in people found that if caries occurs in a primary tooth, the successor tooth is more than twice as likely to have an enamel defect and in the case of early tooth loss for reasons other than trauma such as extraction or infection, the permanent successor tooth was five times more likely to have an enamel defect ( 22 ). In the present study the role of post-operative inflammation vs. trauma to the developing permanent tooth germ during extraction leading to an enamel defect is unknown. There was no apparent post-operative infection and none of the dogs were treated with antibiotics. Hence, infection may not have been a contributing factor to the development of enamel defects in this study, however, this cannot be asserted. We noted the dog’s age at the time of extraction was not significantly correlated with the development of enamel defects. The current published recommendation is to extract linguoverted deciduous mandibular canine teeth as soon as they are observed, which may be as early as 6–8 weeks of age ( 23 ). This recommendation was made to eliminate trauma to the soft tissues of the hard palate and gingiva, release dental interlock which may allow the mandibles to reach their full genetic potential, and to alleviate pain. Although age at the time of extractions and the development of enamel defects was not significant, the fact that 10.4% of dogs age11-12 weeks at the time of extraction and 30.7% of dogs age 8–10 weeks at the time of extraction subsequently developed enamel defects may suggest to wait on extracting deciduous mandibular canine teeth until the dog is older than 11 weeks to minimize the potential for damaging the developing permanent tooth bud. Importantly, it is unknown if a delay in performing extraction of mandibular deciduous canine teeth at 8–10 weeks of age until 11–12 weeks of age or older will affect the ability of the jaws to reach full genetic growth potential due to continued dental interlock and potential interference with skull development. A delay in extractions also has the potential for ongoing palatal trauma and patient discomfort. Timing of extractions is an area where further research is needed to provide a recommendation for the optimal time to perform extractions of deciduous canine teeth and minimize the occurrence of enamel defects in the permanent canine teeth as well as prevent dental interlock which may affect the ability of the jaws to reach their full genetic potential in both length and width ( 24 ). Ultimately, when indicated, the extraction of deciduous teeth should be performed with great care and requires both proper extraction technique and carful operator technique. Pre- and post-extraction dental radiographs are essential when planning extractions to both identify the shape and location of the root and document the presence and location of the developing permanent tooth, as well as any damage present to the developing permanent tooth. The potential for damage to the tooth bud of developing permanent teeth should be discussed with the client prior to interceptive orthodontics ( 25 ). This should include the risks of negative sequelae associated with enamel defects such as increased plaque retention, weakened tooth structure, pulpitis, dentinal sensitivity, and endodontic disease. Finally, it should be noted that 37.8% of dogs required additional treatment to prevent the permanent mandibular canine teeth from traumatizing the hard palate. The possibility of additional treatment once the permanent canine teeth erupt should also be clearly communicated to the client. Due to the retrospective nature of this study, evaluations of the 54 patients lost to follow up was not possible. It is possible that patients who were lost to follow up were perceived to be successful and/or functional in nature by their owners and primary care veterinarians and did not warrant further orthodontic or restorative intervention. This may have potentially altered the rate of enamel defects noted following extraction of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth.

[conclusion] Conclusion Extraction of linguoverted deciduous mandibular canine teeth is performed as an “interceptive” orthodontic procedure. However, we noted that those teeth extractions may result in enamel defects on the permanent teeth in 17.5% of dogs and an enamel defect rate of 14.6%. Informing clients that the potential for damage to the developing tooth bud may result in enamel defects on the permanent mandibular canine teeth is warranted.

retrieved_papers.title: Radiographic predictors for maxillary canine impaction.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2015]
[Abstract] The aims of this study were to compare 3-dimensional cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) images of patients with unilaterally impacted canines and to determine the possible radiographic factors for prediction of maxillary canine impactions.
The sample consisted of 65 patients ranging in age from 9.6 to 13.8 years. The patients comprised 43 girls and 22 boys, with a mean age of 12.1 years and a median age of 12.2 years (±SD 1.23 years). Of the impacted maxillary canines, 32 were located on the right side and 33 on the left side. CBCT radiography was available for all patients. The diagnosis of a unilaterally impacted canine was determined from the patients' dental records as a unilateral failure of the canine to erupt at its appropriate site in the dental arch in contrast to normal eruption of the contralateral side. Radiographic follow-up assessment to identify unilateral impaction was performed for 1 year. The radiographic variables generated and the specific features investigated were collected with 3-dimensional CBCT imaging and compared between the impacted and the contralateral maxillary canines.
Statistically significant differences were found between the impacted and nonimpacted canines. Based on these results, clinical relevance and correlations among the variables-canine rotation, canine crown position, canine cusp tip to the midline and to the occlusal plane, canine angle to the midline, and canine angle to the lateral incisor-were considered as variables in a multivariable prediction model. A prediction model using CBCT for canine impaction was established (area under the curve, 0.965; 95% confidence interval, 0.936 to 0.995). Canine crown position, canine cusp tip to the occlusal plane, and canine angulation to the lateral incisor were the relevant predictors in this model.
Prediction of canine impaction based on CBCT was excellent. The probability of canine impaction obtained from the prediction model can help orthodontists to define the optimal intervention method.

[Introduction] Introduction The development of human dentition is a complex and highly coordinated process that involves multiple tissues and functions, yet its precise regulation is only partially understood. 1 Given the complexity of primary eruption, deciduous tooth exfoliation, and permanent tooth eruption, several factors can disrupt the normal sequence or timing, leading to occlusal disturbances. 2 , 3 Abnormalities in the number, morphology, size, position, or eruption pathway and timing, of teeth are frequently observed in orthodontic and pediatric dental patients, as they can significantly affect the dental arches and occlusion. 4 Understanding the etiology of these anomalies is essential to enable early diagnosis and preventive or interceptive measures. 5 Although the causes of certain developmental anomalies are known, most remain unexplained. The interplay between genetic and environmental factors is still debated, but the current consensus favors a predominantly genetic basis, as familial clustering of dental anomalies is often reported. 4 , 5 , 6 Genetic alterations may produce a single dental anomaly or a combination of anomalies. Because the same genetic defect can manifest through different phenotypes, the identification of associative patterns of dental anomalies within a population could provide valuable clues to their genetic origins. 7 The objective of this study was to determine the prevalence of dental anomalies in a large cohort of orthodontic and pediatric dental patients, and to identify patterns of association among these anomalies through hierarchical cluster analysis. These findings may enhance understanding of shared etiological factors, support early detection, and contribute to risk assessment in clinical orthodontic practice.

[introduction] Introduction The development of human dentition is a complex and highly coordinated process that involves multiple tissues and functions, yet its precise regulation is only partially understood. 1 Given the complexity of primary eruption, deciduous tooth exfoliation, and permanent tooth eruption, several factors can disrupt the normal sequence or timing, leading to occlusal disturbances. 2 , 3 Abnormalities in the number, morphology, size, position, or eruption pathway and timing, of teeth are frequently observed in orthodontic and pediatric dental patients, as they can significantly affect the dental arches and occlusion. 4 Understanding the etiology of these anomalies is essential to enable early diagnosis and preventive or interceptive measures. 5 Although the causes of certain developmental anomalies are known, most remain unexplained. The interplay between genetic and environmental factors is still debated, but the current consensus favors a predominantly genetic basis, as familial clustering of dental anomalies is often reported. 4 , 5 , 6 Genetic alterations may produce a single dental anomaly or a combination of anomalies. Because the same genetic defect can manifest through different phenotypes, the identification of associative patterns of dental anomalies within a population could provide valuable clues to their genetic origins. 7 The objective of this study was to determine the prevalence of dental anomalies in a large cohort of orthodontic and pediatric dental patients, and to identify patterns of association among these anomalies through hierarchical cluster analysis. These findings may enhance understanding of shared etiological factors, support early detection, and contribute to risk assessment in clinical orthodontic practice.

[methods] Measurement of method error A subsample of 30 patients was taken randomly, and the radiographs were re-examined by 1 operator, repeating the measurements of each of the parameters: dental age and presence or absence of anomalies (for this purpose the detailed measurements of the variables studied were again taken). The same systematic approach was followed the first time, aiming to estimate the measurement error and evaluate intra-observer reproducibility. The Dahlberg formula was used to estimate the error of the dental age measurement method (X) ( Figure 1 ), particularised to the 33 double measurements of the current analysis. The relative error of the method was also calculated as the quotient between d and the mean of the variable involved and expressed as a percentage. The Kappa concordance index was used to estimate the method's reproducibility for the variables measured. Fig. 1 Dahlberg's formula. Fig 1 Graph 1 Sampling distribution of age by sex. Graph 1 For dental age, the difference between the first and second measurements was calculated. The mean difference was 0.073 ± 0.169 years, with a 95% confidence interval (0.016, 0.131). Since the interval does not contain zero (by a small margin), it can be concluded that the first measurement tended to measure a somewhat older age than the second. Dahlberg' s d was put at 0.132 years, which is an estimate of the error of the age measurement method. Relativising the D (to the magnitude of what is being measured), a relative error of 1.22% was obtained, indicating reasonably good reproducibility. With respect to the variables indicating the presence or absence of anomalies, the Kappa concordance index obtained a constant value of 1 for all of them. Consequently, it was possible to conclude an absolute reproducibility in the anomaly identification method.

[results] Results After applying the inclusion and exclusion criteria, a total of 1,904 panoramic radiographs were selected. The prevalence of dental anomalies in the sample studied was evaluated ( Graph 3 ), as well as the prevalence according to sex ( Table 1 ) and, finally, the relationship of these anomalies with dental age in the sample ( Graph 4 ). Table 1 Prevalence of dental anomalies according to sex in the studied sample Table 1 Sex Total Female Male N % N % N % Primary Anomaly Total 1904 100.00% 1123 100.00% 781 100.00% Agenesis 5-5 94 4.94% 54 4.81% 40 5.12% Agenesis 5+5 37 1.94% 23 2.05% 14 1.79% Agenesis 2+2 177 9.30% 110 9.80% 67 8.58% Other Agenesis 31 1.63% 16 1.42% 15 1.92% Impaction 3+3 210 11.03% 127 11.31% 83 10.63% Impaction 3-3 10 0.53% 5 0.45% 5 0.64% Incisors Impaction 33 1.73% 12 1.07% 21 2.69% Bicuspids Impaction 42 2.21% 25 2.23% 17 2.18% Blockage 3+3 111 5.83% 65 5.79% 46 5.89% Maxillary Transposition C-1 st Bicuspid 15 0.79% 9 0.80% 6 0.77% Maxillary Transposition C-LI 6 0.32% 3 0.27% 3 0.38% Other Maxillary Transposition 4 0.21% 3 0.27% 1 0.13% Mandibular Transposition C-LI 3 0.16% 1 0.09% 2 0.26% 1 st M Ectopic Eruption 40 2.10% 19 1.69% 21 2.69% Ankylosis 15 0.79% 12 1.07% 3 0.38% Impaction 7-7 55 2.89% 33 2.94% 22 2.82% Mandibular M PFE 19 1.00% 11 0.98% 8 1.02% Mesiodens 14 0.74% 5 0.45% 9 1.15% Supernumerary 14 0.74% 9 0.80% 5 0.64% 5-5 : Lower second bicuspids; 5+5 : Upper second bicuspids; 2+2 : Upper lateral incisors; 3+3 : Upper canines; 3-3 : Lower canines; C : Canine; LI : Lateral incisor; CI : Central incisor; M: Molar; 7-7 : Lower second molars; PFE : Primary failure eruption. Hyperodontia The prevalence of hyperodontia in the sample was 1.47% (28 cases). It can be estimated, with 95% confidence, that the true prevalence in the population is between the limits 0.93 and 2.01%. The prevalence of mesiodens and other supernumerary teeth was the same (0.74%), the upper lateral incisors and lower right premolars being the most affected by hyperodontia (0.11% respectively each). There were no significant differences between sexes. However, regarding mesiodens, applying the Wilcoxon test due to its low prevalence in the sample, significant differences were found ( Z = 0.046 ) when comparing the relationship of dental age in individuals with anomaly versus individuals without anomaly with the same chronological age and sex, with the dental age being higher in the sample that presented mesiodens (7.69 ± 1.69). Hypodontia The prevalence of hypodontia in the sample was 14.97% (285 subjects), with an estimate of the actual prevalence in the population of between 13.37 and 16.57%. There was no sexual dimorphism. The prevalence of agenesis (AG) of the lower second premolars (5-5) in the sample was 4.94%, one of the 4 most frequent anomalies in hypodontia, with no preference for bilateral or unilateral right and left). There was a certain tendency towards a delay in dental age in individuals with this anomaly, which was statistically significant ( P = .001 ). The upper second premolars (5+5) had a lower prevalence in the sample than the lower ones, 1.94%, and there was a preference for bilateral cases (1.16%). Hypodontia of upper lateral incisors (2+2), agenesis and microdontic incisors, was the most frequent anomaly in the sample, being observed in 177 individuals (9.3%). This delay was recorded as significant concerning dental age ( P = .017 ). Other agenesis, different from the above, was found in 31 individuals (1.63%), including agenesis of the lower right incisor (0.21%) or the upper left first premolar (4+4) (0.16%). Impactions Within this group of anomalies, the most frequently observed, and second in frequency of all the anomalies, was palatal impaction of the upper canines (3+3), found in 175 patients (9.19% of the sample), with a prevalence of bilateral cases. There was no significance in terms of delay in dental age in the presence of this impaction ( P = .157). Vestibular impaction of the upper canines (3+3) had a much lower prevalence in the sample (1.84%), and bilateral cases were also the most numerous (0.79%). There was a certain tendency to a delay in dental age in these patients, although not significant ( P < .1). Only 10 individuals of the sample (0.53%) had lower canines (3-3) impacted, and there were no differences in dental age between them and individuals who did not present such impaction. The prevalence of impacted incisors was 1.73%, of which the majority were upper incisors (1.68%), since only one subject had impacted lower incisors (0.05%), making this the anomaly with the lowest prevalence in the sample. Among the impacted upper incisors, the most numerous were the central incisors (1+1). Unlike the rest of the anomalies, a higher prevalence of impaction was observed in the upper incisors in men ( P = .004 ), while in the lower incisors it can be accepted as homogeneous ( Graph 2 ). Graph 2 Prevalence of incisor impaction according to sex. *p<0.01 Graph 2 Graph 3 Prevalence of dental anomalies. 5-5 : Lower second bicuspids; 5+5 : Upper second bicuspids; 3+3 : Upper canines; 3-3 : Lower canines; MAX : Maxillary; C : Canine; LI : Lateral incisor; CI : Central incisor; MDB : Mandibular; 7-7 : Lower second molars; PFE : Primary failure eruption. Graph 3 Graph 4 Mean difference in dental age between groups with and without anomalies. * P < ,05; ** P < ,01. 5-5 : Lower second bicuspids; 5+5 : Upper second bicuspids; 3+3 : Upper canines; 3-3 : Lower canines; MAX : Maxillary; MDB : Mandibular; 7-7 : Lower second molars; PFE : Primary failure eruption; * P < .05 ; ** P < .01 (IC 95%). Graph 4 Impacted premolars are the second most frequent type of impaction in the sample (2.21%), with the prevalence of impacted second premolars much higher (2.14%) than that of impacted first premolars (0.11%). The mean dental age in the cases presenting premolar impaction was 9.04 ± 2.11 years, while in the control subgroup, it was 9.52 ± 2.31. There was a statistically significant delay in dental age for this anomaly ( P = .005). Regarding the second molar, all the impactions were recorded in the mandible, with a prevalence of 2.89%. They presented with a similar frequency on the right and left sides and more frequently bilaterally (1.37%). There was no evidence of delayed dental age in the presence of this anomaly ( P = .359). Blockage of the upper canines The upper canines (3+3) were found blocked in 111 subjects of the sample (5.83%), thus being the third most frequent anomaly. Most of the cases observed were bilateral (3.68%). No differences were found between sexes. Its relationship with dental age could not be considered significant ( P = .653). Transpositions The sample prevalence of transpositions was 1.42%, most of which were upper transpositions (1.26% vs. 0.16% of lower transpositions). Regarding the type, the most frequent transposition was that of the upper canine with the first premolar, followed by the upper canine with the lateral incisor, and that of the lower canine with the lateral incisor. There were no significant differences between the sexes. Regarding the transposition of the upper canine with the first premolar, given that the sample of this anomaly was small, the analysis using the Wilcoxon test indicated that there were no differences in the distribution of dental age in the 2 subgroups (with and without transposition) (Z = -1.491). For the rest of the transpositions, the use of any type of statistical test was ruled out because they were anomalies with very low prevalence. Transmigrations No patient with transmigration was observed in the sample. Ankylosis of primary molars This anomaly was observed in 15 individuals of the sample (0.79%), with no differences between sexes. The second primary molars had the highest prevalence (0.67%), being very similar in both arches. In most cases (0.46%) there were 2 or more ankylosed primary molars. No differences were found in the distribution of the dental age of the group with ankylosed primary molars and those paired with the same. Ectopic eruption of upper permanent first molar teeth The prevalence of this anomaly in the sample was 2.10%, and the actual prevalence in the population can be estimated between 1.46 and 2.75%. More than half of the cases were bilateral (1.26%). The mean delay recorded in individuals with ectopic eruption of upper permanent first molars (6+6) was -0.33 ± 1.10 years, a difference that suggested a powerful trend, although it was not statistically significant ( P = .062 ). Primary failure of eruption of lower molars This anomaly was observed only in the lower arch. It had a sample prevalence of 1.00%. It was most frequently observed in the second permanent molars (0.79%) and almost half of the cases were bilateral (0.47%). The difference in dental age with the group without anomalies indicated that it was advanced for the subgroup of cases, but this difference could not be accepted as significant (Z < 0.1). Multivariate cluster analysis In 672 subjects of the sample (35.3%) at least one anomaly was observed, and among them almost a quarter of the total sample (24.9%) presented only one, while in the remaining cases (10.4%) 2 or more anomalies were recorded. No individual presented more than 5 anomalies. On these 197 individuals presenting at least 2 dental anomalies, a hierarchical cluster analysis was performed, a procedure that classified 82.7% of them into 5 different segments. The first 3 segments (1 to 3) included 69% of the individuals in the subsample, and the next 2 (4 and 5) comprised 13.7%. The "sixth segment" brought together the cases that were unclassifiable (17.3%), because their similarity to every one of the segments obtained was so small that it did not allow their aggregation ( Table 2 ). : Lower canines; C : Canine; LI : Lateral incisor; CI : Central incisor; M: Molar; 7-7 : Lower second molars; PFE : Primary failure eruption. Segment 1 (S1) consisted of 70 individuals and was characterized by the association between upper lateral incisor hypodontia (84.3%) and palatal impaction of upper canines (72.9%). Since approximately 75% of the subjects included in this block had one of these 2 anomalies, both were associated in a high percentage of individuals (at least 50%). In segment 2 (S2), composed of 34 individuals, the presence of the different agenesis studied stood out. Lower second premolars dominated (88% in the segment, compared to 25.9% in the general group), which was associated with agenesis of upper second premolars (58.8%), hypodontia of upper lateral incisors (52.9%), and "other agenesis" (23.5%). Segment 3 (S3), with 32 individuals, was mainly characterized by the association of upper canine blockage (68.8%) with lower permanent second molar impaction (56.3%). In segment 4 (S4), formed by 14 individuals, the presence of upper canines impacted in the vestibular region predominated (92.9%), which in turn had a high association with hypodontia of the upper lateral incisors (50%). Notably, of the 3 subjects in the working sample in which "other maxillary transpositions" were recorded, 2 were grouped in this block. In segment 5, composed of 13 individuals, 3 anomalies were mainly associated: palatal impaction of upper canines (61.5%), impaction of premolars (69.2%), and ectopic eruption of upper permanent first molars (61.5%). Patterns of association of dental anomalies Initially, the 4 anomalies with the highest prevalence were analysed: agenesis of lower second premolars, hypodontia of upper lateral incisors, palatal impaction of upper canines, and blockage of upper canines ( Table 3 ). There is a higher prevalence of hypodontia of upper lateral incisors in the subgroup in which the primary anomaly was agenesis of lower second premolars than in the control group (22.73% and 9.67% respectively), a difference that was statistically significant ( p = .007 ). This indicates an association between both anomalies, but only in this sense; it is not reciprocal since the prevalence of agenesis of lower second premolars in the primary subgroup of upper lateral incisor hypodontia (6.82%) does not differ significantly from the presence of this anomaly in the control group (4%). A significant association ( P < .001 ) was also found between upper lateral incisor hypodontia, as a primary anomaly, and palatal impaction of upper canines, which in that subgroup was observed in 29.55% of the individuals, while in the control group it was recorded in 7.67% of the cases. This relationship was also one-way, as the prevalence of upper lateral incisor hypodontia in the canine impaction subgroup is similar to the same in the control group (9.09% and 9.67%). Subsequently, the analysis was extended to the twelve most frequent anomalies ( Table 4 , Table 5 ). When the primary anomaly was agenesis of the lower second premolars, agenesis of the upper second premolars was present in 10% of the cases, compared to only 1% in the control group, making the result statistically significant ( P = .042 ). The reciprocal relationship was also significant, as this relationship between anomalies was one of the most intense of all those contrasted ( P < .001 ). Table 5 Primary Anomaly Agenesis 5-5 Agenesis 5+5 Agenesis 2+2 P Impact 3+3 V Impact 3+3 Incisor Impaction Bicuspid Impaction Blockage 3+3 Transpositions Ectopic Eruption 6+6 Impaction 7+7 Supernumerary Primary Anomaly Agenesis 5-5 0.042 0.069 0.686 1.000 1.000 0.382 0.583 0.174 1.000 0.125 1.000 Agenesis 5+5 <0,001 0.257 1.000 0.382 1.000 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.125 1.000 1.000 Agenesis 2+2 0,.038 1,000 0.010 0.382 1.000 1.000 0.583 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.319 P Impact 3+3 0.492 0.250 0.257 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.227 0.022 1.000 0.439 1.000 V Impact 3+3 1.000 1.000 0.069 1.000 1.000 0.382 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.439 1.000 Incisor Impaction 0.492 1.000 0.701 1.000 0.018 1.000 0.583 1.000 0.439 1,.000 <0.001 Bicuspid Impaction 0.492 1.000 0.257 0.403 1.000 0.382 0.015 1.000 0.125 0.439 1.000 Blockage 3+3 1.000 1.000 0.701 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.382 1.000 1.000 0.439 1.000 Transpositions 0.038 0.250 0.069 0.107 0.382 0.382 1.000 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.319 Ectopic Eruption 6+6 1.000 1.000 0.701 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.018 0.583 1.000 1.000 1.000 Impaction 7-7 0.492 1.000 1.000 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.382 0.066 1.000 1.000 1.000 Supernumerary 1.000 1.000 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.003 0.382 0.227 0.002 0.439 1.000 5-5 : Lower second bicuspids; 5+5 : Upper second bicuspids; 2+2 : Upper lateral incisors; 3+3 : Upper canines; 6+6: Upper first molars; 7-7 : Lower second molars When upper lateral incisor hypodontia was considered the primary anomaly, the prevalence of lower second premolar agenesis was 15%, which was statistically significant ( P = .038 ). Likewise, a reciprocal relationship was observed between incisor impaction and the presence of supernumerary teeth. In the subgroup where said impaction was the primary anomaly, 30% of the cases also presented hypodontia, compared to 1.5% of individuals with the latter anomaly in the control group; this result is statistically significant ( P < .001 ). Other significant associations were observed and verified only in one direction: The frequency of transpositions recorded in the subgroup whose primary anomaly was hypodontia ( P = .002 ). In the presence of upper lateral incisor hypodontia, palatal impaction of upper canines obtained a significantly higher prevalence than 8.5% in the control group ( p=0.01 ); Incisor impaction was significantly related ( P = .018 ) with vestibular impaction of upper canines; The association between ectopic eruption of upper first molars as a primary anomaly with premolar impaction was significant ( P = .018); In association with palatal impaction of upper canines, the presence of transpositions was also significant ( P = .022 ); in turn, transpositions being the primary anomaly, the frequency of agenesis of lower second premolars was 10.2%, significantly higher than that presented in the controls (3.0%) ( P = .038 ). Finally, associations were observed that did not reach the level of significance. Still, when they had a value of P <.07, it was considered that they indicated a tendency for the presence of these relationships. Thus, the frequency of upper canine blockage is increased in the subgroup of lower second molar impaction ( P = .066 ). On the other hand, upper lateral incisor hypodontia is more likely to appear when there is also palatal impaction of the upper canines ( P = .069 ) or some transposition ( P = .069 ).

[discussion] Discussion The development of human dentition is governed by a complex interplay of genetic and environmental factors, which determine tooth number, morphology, size, and eruption sequence. 20 Alterations in these developmental processes often result in dental anomalies that not only affect the esthetic and functional aspects of occlusion but also complicate orthodontic treatment planning. 7 The present study, based on a large retrospective sample of 1,904 patients, aimed to evaluate the prevalence of various dental anomalies and to identify patterns of co-occurrence that may indicate a shared genetic etiology.

[conclusion] Conclusion In the analysed population, 35.5% presented at least one dental anomaly, with hypodontia of upper lateral incisors and palatal impaction of upper canines being the most prevalent abnormalities. Upper incisor impaction was more frequently recorded in men than in women, a difference that reached statistical significance. A relationship was also identified between certain anomalies and dental age, with agenesis of lower second premolars, hypodontia of upper lateral incisors, and premolar impaction being associated with delayed dental maturation, while the presence of mesiodens was associated with advanced dental maturation. More than one anomaly was found in 10.4% of individuals, and the analysis of associative patterns allowed the establishment of 5 distinct segments, with the first 3 accounting for 69% of the cases with abnormalities. In particular, the first segment was characterised by the coexistence of hypodontia of upper lateral incisors and palatal impaction of canines. The second, due to the presence of multiple agenesis, predominantly in upper and lower second premolars and upper lateral incisors; and the third, due to the association of blocked upper canines with impacted lower second molars. Overall, the findings of this study expand the available knowledge about the prevalence and association patterns of dental anomalies and constitute a starting point for future research aimed at clarifying their possible genetic origin.

retrieved_papers.title: Outcome of Surgical Extraction of Linguoverted Deciduous Mandibular Canine Teeth, Performed as an Interceptive Orthodontic Procedure in Puppies, on Permanent Occlusion.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2023]
[Abstract] Linguoversion of deciduous mandibular canine teeth can be a painful condition, interferes with the development and growth of the jaws, and potentially leads to further malocclusions affecting permanent dentition. Extraction of linguoverted deciduous mandibular canines is considered an interceptive orthodontic procedure that would allow unimpeded development of the jaws and permanent teeth. This study assessed clinical records of 124 dogs that had linguoverted deciduous mandibular canine teeth surgically extracted between October 2010 and September 2019 in a veterinary dental referral clinic. Seventy-seven cases fulfilled the study criteria. Fifty-one percent of these patients required further orthodontic treatment of the permanent occlusion and forty-nine percent demonstrated atraumatic permanent occlusion. The study found no correlation of the outcome with age at the time of surgery. The class of malocclusion (class 1 or class 2) at the time of surgery was also not associated with the outcome.

[Introduction] Introduction Interceptive orthodontics is a proactive orthodontic treatment that is presumed to allow for the development of normal permanent occlusion in puppies exhibiting malocclusion ( 1 , 2 ). For this study classification of malocclusions were made according to American Veterinary Dental College (AVDC) Nomenclature. The AVDC defines a class 1 malocclusion as neutroclusion with normal rostrocaudal relationship of the maxillary and mandibular dental arches and malposition of one or more individual teeth and class 2 malocclusion as mandibular distoclusion with an abnormal rostrocaudal relationship between the dental arches in which the mandibular arch occludes caudal to its normal position relative to the maxillary arch ( 3 ). In this study interceptive orthodontics involved extraction of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth in patients with a class 1 or class 2 malocclusion and linguoverted canine teeth ( Figure 1 ). Extraction of deciduous mandibular canine teeth should be performed with an abundance of caution to prevent damage to the developing permanent canine tooth. Given that enamel development ceases prior to or at the time of tooth eruption, enamel in dogs should be fully developed in permanent teeth 15–31.4 weeks after birth ( 4 ). This period of development makes enamel susceptible to injury from a variety of causes both environmental and systemic. Figure 1 A class 2 malocclusion and linguoversion of the deciduous mandibular canine teeth (white arrow) in an 11-week-old dog resulting in trauma to the hard palate. Most enamel defects are typically caused by trauma, infection, or inflammation resulting in a disruption during tooth development ( 4 ). Traumatic damage to the dental organ during development may result in enamel hypoplasia or hypocalcification, characterized by defects that are localized, irregular, and often affecting a single tooth ( 5 ). When enamel hypoplasia or enamel defects affect only one tooth they are known as a Turner’s tooth or exhibiting Turner’s hypoplasia ( 6 ). Likewise, systemic processes, such as fever, distemper, hypocalcemia, nutritional deficiencies, excessive intake of fluoride, and some drugs taken during the period of enamel formation, may result in enamel dysplasia. In these cases, all teeth developing at that time are affected. Hypoplasia is defined as a quantitative defect of enamel visually and is histomorphologically identified as an external defect involving the surface of the enamel and associated with reduced thickness of enamel ( 7 , 8 ). The defective enamel may occur as shallow or deep pits or rows of pits arranged horizontally, or as small or large, wide or narrow grooves. Silberman et al. ( 9 ) created a simplified hypoplasia index ( Table 1 ) which was used to characterize enamel hypoplasia of the affected teeth in this study. In the present study, all dogs had type-IV enamel hypoplasia due to trauma during extraction or post-operative inflammation resulting in brown and yellow enamel discoloration, abnormal coalescence, and missing enamel with the majority having defects on the lateral or occlusal aspect of the permanent canine teeth ( Figures 2 , 3 ). Table 1 Simplified tooth hypoplasia index ( 9 ). Type I hypoplasia: enamel discoloration due to hypoplasia Type II hypoplasia: abnormal coalescence due to hypoplasia Type III hypoplasia: missing some parts of enamel due to hypoplasia Type IV hypoplasia: a combination of previous three types of hypoplasia Figure 2 Enamel defects on a permanent right mandibular canine tooth and the right mandibular 3rd incisor (Black arrows). Figure 3 Enamel defect on a permanent right mandibular canine tooth (white arrow). The close anatomical relationship of the apex of the deciduous tooth to the developing permanent tooth bud explains the potential for possible developmental disturbances during extraction ( Figure 4 ). A cephalometric study in people demonstrated the thickness of the hard tissue barrier between primary incisor teeth and their successors was <3 mm ( 10 ). However, no such data regarding the barrier between the deciduous mandibular canine teeth and the developing tooth buds exists for dogs which may have assisted in avoiding the potential disruptive effect of extractions on permanent tooth buds during odontogenesis. Deciduous canine teeth in dogs are long, thin, and fragile and operator technique during extraction may significantly traumatize the developing permanent tooth. Gentle tissue handling, and good hand control are essential while extracting the deciduous teeth. In addition, if a deciduous canine tooth fractures during extraction, removal of the root tip is crucial, because retained root tips may alter the eruption of the permanent tooth, and may lead to infection, further potentiating the formation of enamel defects. Prior to extracting a deciduous tooth, obtaining preoperative dental radiographs is essential to determine the shape and location of the root and to document the presence and location of the developing permanent tooth ( 11 ). Because of the close three-dimensional spatial anatomic relationship between the apex of primary teeth and the developing permanent tooth the use of a cone beam CT (CBCT) scan is beneficial in assessing more precisely the exact spatial relationship of the crown and the apex in relation to the permanent successor ( 12 ). Regardless of the extraction technique employed, post-extraction dental radiographs should be obtained to document complete removal of the deciduous canine and to potentially identify damage to the developing tooth bud. Figure 4 Dental radiograph of the left mandibular deciduous canine tooth (white arrow) and the developing permanent canine tooth (white arrowhead). Note the proximity of the apex of a deciduous mandibular canine to the crown of a developing permanent canine tooth. The incidence and rate of enamel defects on permanent dentition following extraction of deciduous teeth in dogs has not been reported. Hence, the primary objective of this retrospective study was to determine an incident rate of enamel defects occurring following extraction of linguoverted mandibular deciduous canine teeth in young dogs which were causing palatal trauma, dental interlock, and signs of oral pain.

In these cases, all teeth developing at that time are affected. Hypoplasia is defined as a quantitative defect of enamel visually and is histomorphologically identified as an external defect involving the surface of the enamel and associated with reduced thickness of enamel ( 7 , 8 ). The defective enamel may occur as shallow or deep pits or rows of pits arranged horizontally, or as small or large, wide or narrow grooves. Silberman et al. ( 9 ) created a simplified hypoplasia index ( Table 1 ) which was used to characterize enamel hypoplasia of the affected teeth in this study. In the present study, all dogs had type-IV enamel hypoplasia due to trauma during extraction or post-operative inflammation resulting in brown and yellow enamel discoloration, abnormal coalescence, and missing enamel with the majority having defects on the lateral or occlusal aspect of the permanent canine teeth ( Figures 2 , 3 ). Table 1 Simplified tooth hypoplasia index ( 9 ). Type I hypoplasia: enamel discoloration due to hypoplasia Type II hypoplasia: abnormal coalescence due to hypoplasia Type III hypoplasia: missing some parts of enamel due to hypoplasia Type IV hypoplasia: a combination of previous three types of hypoplasia Figure 2 Enamel defects on a permanent right mandibular canine tooth and the right mandibular 3rd incisor (Black arrows). Figure 3 Enamel defect on a permanent right mandibular canine tooth (white arrow). The close anatomical relationship of the apex of the deciduous tooth to the developing permanent tooth bud explains the potential for possible developmental disturbances during extraction ( Figure 4 ). A cephalometric study in people demonstrated the thickness of the hard tissue barrier between primary incisor teeth and their successors was <3 mm ( 10 ). However, no such data regarding the barrier between the deciduous mandibular canine teeth and the developing tooth buds exists for dogs which may have assisted in avoiding the potential disruptive effect of extractions on permanent tooth buds during odontogenesis. Deciduous canine teeth in dogs are long, thin, and fragile and operator technique during extraction may significantly traumatize the developing permanent tooth. Gentle tissue handling, and good hand control are essential while extracting the deciduous teeth. In addition, if a deciduous canine tooth fractures during extraction, removal of the root tip is crucial, because retained root tips may alter the eruption of the permanent tooth, and may lead to infection, further potentiating the formation of enamel defects. Prior to extracting a deciduous tooth, obtaining preoperative dental radiographs is essential to determine the shape and location of the root and to document the presence and location of the developing permanent tooth ( 11 ). Because of the close three-dimensional spatial anatomic relationship between the apex of primary teeth and the developing permanent tooth the use of a cone beam CT (CBCT) scan is beneficial in assessing more precisely the exact spatial relationship of the crown and the apex in relation to the permanent successor ( 12 ). Regardless of the extraction technique employed, post-extraction dental radiographs should be obtained to document complete removal of the deciduous canine and to potentially identify damage to the developing tooth bud. Figure 4 Dental radiograph of the left mandibular deciduous canine tooth (white arrow) and the developing permanent canine tooth (white arrowhead). Note the proximity of the apex of a deciduous mandibular canine to the crown of a developing permanent canine tooth. The incidence and rate of enamel defects on permanent dentition following extraction of deciduous teeth in dogs has not been reported. Hence, the primary objective of this retrospective study was to determine an incident rate of enamel defects occurring following extraction of linguoverted mandibular deciduous canine teeth in young dogs which were causing palatal trauma, dental interlock, and signs of oral pain.

[methods] Methods and materials Medical records from five veterinary specialty dentistry and oral surgery practices were reviewed for dogs who underwent deciduous mandibular canine teeth extraction due to linguoverted canine teeth between January 2017 and December 2021. Dogs from 38 breeds included in the study were between 8 and 12 weeks of age at the time of surgery and had class 1 or class 2 malocclusions with linguoverted mandibular canine teeth. All dogs underwent surgical extractions of deciduous mandibular canine teeth (i.e., an open extraction technique) and returned for at least one in-person recheck exam to evaluate the permanent mandibular canine teeth. Dogs were excluded from the study if they were older than 12 weeks of age at the time of extraction, had deciduous incisor teeth extracted at the same time as the deciduous canine teeth, did not undergo an in-person recheck exam, or were lost to follow up. Patient information collected during original presentation included patient history, signalment, physical exam findings, oral exam findings including descriptions and photographs of the malocclusion. Information collected during recheck exams included written descriptions and photographs of enamel defects in permanent mandibular canine teeth and whether patients required additional treatment to prevent the permanent canine teeth from causing palatal and/or gingival trauma. Surgical teeth extraction was performed as previously described ( 13 ). Briefly, a sulcular incision was made followed by raising a triangle mucosal flap using a periosteal elevator. Partial buccal alveolectomy was performed using a round burr on a high-speed handpiece followed by luxating the tooth using appropriate size luxator. Delivery of the tooth was done using extraction forceps ( 13 ). All surgical incisions were closed using an absorbable monofilament suture material in a simple interrupted fashion. Pre and post extraction radiographs were obtained for all patients included in this study. Statistical analysis Statistical analyses were performed using SAS 9.4 (Cary, NC). A significance threshold of p < 0.05 was used. The assumption of normality for age was evaluated via inspection of QQ- and PP-plots, histograms, and skewness. Age was determined to not be normally distributed variables were summarized descriptively with median and interquartile range (IQR). Frequencies were reported as both numbers and percentages with 95% CIs where appropriate. Univariable logistic regression analyses were used to test for association of patient sex and age with odds of having an enamel defect. Log-likelihood p -values and odds ratios with profile-likelihood odds ratio confidence limits were reported.

[results] Results The search of medical records from five veterinary dental specialty practices for dogs between the age of 8–12 weeks that underwent surgical extraction of deciduous mandibular canine teeth yielded 128 cases. Fifty-four cases were not reexamined following extraction and were not included in the study. Therefore, the final group of patients included in the study was 74 individual dogs where extraction of mandibular deciduous canine teeth was performed due to linguoverted mandibular canine teeth. The 74 dogs included in the study represented 38 breeds with standard poodles (9.5%, 7/74), poodle and poodle crosses (including ‘doodle’ breeds) (21.6%, 6/74), and Labradors (6.7%, 5/74) being the most represented.
