Since canine eruption is closely linked to the root’s mineralization stage, it serves as a reliable developmental reference [ 25 ]. Additionally, the canine germ is located distal to the cleft and, unlike incisors, does not exhibit associated morphological aberrations, making it a reliable reference for root mineralization assessment [ 26 ]. The bone graft height and limbus alveolaris were evaluated relative to the canine crown. OPTGs obtained at T0, T1, and T2 were assessed following the method described by Aurouze et al. [ 27 ] and Long et al. [ 19 ]. The canine angulation in the mesiodistal dimension was assessed using the method outlined by Dausch-Neumann [ 20 ]. Modern X-ray devices equipped with positioning aids and staffs trained in their use ensure standardization and reproducibility of imaging. Positioning errors during OPTG acquisition were minimized by strict inclusion criteria and exclusion of low-quality images [ 18 ]. Despite the limitations of 2D imaging, previous studies have shown sufficient accuracy for assessing SABG outcomes [ 28 , 29 ]. A continuous bone bridge of about 9 mm is considered essential for successful tooth eruption into the graft and effective orthodontic movement. Thus, precise radiologic examination around the erupting canine, in relation to individual dental development, is crucial for optimizing SABG timing [ 30 , 31 ]. However, over the last years, CT and CBCT have become increasingly crucial for the assessment of cleft regions and bone grafts, enabling precise volumetric analysis, improving treatment planning and postoperative outcome assessment [ 32 , 33 ]. Conventional 2D OPTGs often underestimate bone resorption [ 34 ] as they only capture the vertical dimensions. In this study, bucco-palatal resorption could not be accurately assessed from OPTGs. Resorption may reach up to 49,5% within the first year after surgery [ 35 , 36 ], highlighting the importance of evaluating the bucco-palatal bone thickness to ensure proper canine positioning. Our study relies on conventional radiographic measurements while acknowledging the limitations of volumetric assessment compared to CBCT. Although 3D imaging offers superior assessment compared to 2D, a consistent imaging protocol is necessary to ensure comparability across centers [ 37 ]. Furthermore, adherence to the ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) principle remains crucial, particularly in pediatric patients. Although studies acknowledge the surgical advantages of preoperative CBCT scans [ 38 ], recent research shows no clear predictive benefits of CBCT over conventional intraoral radiographs for assessing canine eruption following SABG [ 39 ]. Thus, advanced imaging should only be considered when its clinical advantages outweigh the risk of radiation exposure. SABG, introduced by Boyne and Sands in 1972 [ 40 ], is typically performed during the late mixed dentition (8–12 years of age) and has become the standard treatment for reconstructing bony defects in CLP patients [ 41 ]. After age eight, maxillary growth is primarily completed, reducing the risk of growth impairment following SABG [ 42 – 44 ]. Bergland et al. [ 45 ] demonstrated optimal outcomes when SABG was performed before the canine eruption. In our cohort, only three out of 127 cleft areas were grafted before the age of eight, making growth effects unlikely to occur. Most patients (57%) underwent SABG between the ages of 9 and 11 (mean age: 9 ± 1,6 years), consistent with the previous reports (8,2 to 10,6 years) [ 35 , 40 – 43 ]. Impact of SABG timing on space closure SABG timing significantly influenced space closure outcomes in patients with lateral incisor agenesis. Early SABG ( R ≤ 0,5) tripled the success rate of orthodontic space closures compared to late SABG ( R ≥ 0,75), as the mesial drift of the erupting canine ( R ≤ 0,5) decreased the canine inclination angle, and minimized orthodontic movement (Student’s t = 2,931). The required mesial movement for space closure ranged from only 1,7 mm ( R = 0,25) to 6,2 mm ( R = 1,0). Thus, early SABG ( R ≤ 0,5) facilitates successful orthodontic space closure in CLP patients with lateral incisor agenesis. The absence or presence of lateral incisors and the mesio-distal space must be considered in decision-making on the type of space closure [ 46 ]. Although the aesthetic outcomes of both orthodontic and prosthodontic space closure are satisfactory and comparable in the long term, the risk of cranio-mandibular dysfunction increases after prosthodontic rehabilitation with resin-bonded bridges [ 45 ]. Despite conventional SABG being well documented and considered the gold standard, the current literature suggests a shift towards an earlier intervention before the eruption of the lateral incisors [ 47 , 48 ]. Early SABG (around 5–7 years) may offer particular advantages for patients with CLP with lateral incisors, as it provides adequate bone support for their eruption [ 44 ]. Evidence, although limited, suggests that early SABG does not impair maxillary growth [ 9 , 45 , 49 ] and may yield superior outcomes, including significantly higher lateral incisor survival, improved bone graft volume [ 49 ] and better periodontal health [ 47 ]. Cephalometric and volumetric studies have shown that both groups exhibited similar maxillary growth patterns [ 50 ] between early and conventional SABG (around the age of ten), while some report greater bone preservation when grafting is performed earlier [ 40 , 50 ]. Precious et al. [ 51 ] showed better periodontal health, crown length, and central incisor symmetry with early SABG. Importantly, regardless of the age at which the ABG is placed, the eruption of a permanent tooth (lateral incisor or canine) into the graft has a positive and stabilizing effect on bone height [ 40 ]. Bone graft and limbus alveolaris height This study highlights the impact of SABG timing on bone graft preservation in patients with lateral incisor agenesis. Late SABG ( R = 1) showed 3,5 mm more resorption compared to early SABG ( R = 0,25). These findings align with those of Bergland et al. [ 7 ], who reported that 64% of early SABG cases had preserved interdental septum height, compared to only 37% of late SABG cases (those performed before and after canine eruption, respectively). As septum height is crucial for future dental implants, early SABG may be preferable when orthodontic space closure is not indicated. These findings align with previous studies [ 7 , 52 ] that recommend SABG near the eruption of the tooth adjacent to the cleft, followed by early loading through orthodontic loading or implant placement, to prevent functional bone atrophy. Zhang et al. reported maximum bone graft reduction within three to six months post-SABG [ 43 ]. However, as CBCT was not yet standard, our assessments relied mainly on conventional 2D radiographs. Recent 3D studies emphasized the prognostic value of preoperative cleft volume in determining grafting success. Larger cleft defects have been consistently associated with increased risk of graft failure, stressing the need for precise volumetric assessment to guide treatment planning [ 53 ]. This has direct implications for graft material selection. Structural bone blocks, harvested from the iliac crest and chin, generally yield superior outcomes compared to cancellous bone [ 54 ]. In contrast, for smaller cleft defects, synthetic materials are gaining relevance. A recent evaluation of a bioactive glass material (GlassBONE™) demonstrated satisfactory bone fill, favorable periodontal health, and successful eruption patterns, supporting its potential as a minimally invasive alternative in selected pediatric patients [ 55 ]. Canine impaction and eruption Compared to the 1–2% prevalence of canine impaction in non-cleft populations [ 56 ], CLP patients are at a greater risk of cleft-sided canine impaction, with reports ranging between 6% and 56% [ 57 – 59 ]. In the present study, the overall impaction rate of cleft-sided canines was 10,2%, which falls within the abovementioned range. Notably, the majority (89,8%) of cleft-sided canines erupted spontaneously into the grafted alveolar region, revealing a positive effect of SABG on successful canine eruption, which aligns with the findings of a recent CBCT-based review [ 60 ]. Of the 127 cleft-sided canines evaluated, 13 were impacted, with three necessitating surgical removal. Notably, all of these cases occurred in patients with lateral incisor agenesis, underscoring the increased risk in this subgroup. Early SABG ( R ≤ 0,5) was associated with an increasing impaction rate, consistent with the findings of Hoang et al. [ 58 ]. This trend was particularly evident in agenesis cases, which accounted for 76,9% of the impacted canines; more than three times the rate observed in patients with non-agenesis of lateral incisors. Although early SABG ( R < 0.5) was associated with increased impaction risk, this can be mitigated through close radiographic surveillance and timely interceptive orthodontic treatment. Early detection of eruption deviations is essential for guiding canine eruption. Additionally, lateral incisor agenesis is associated with palatinal displacement of the erupting canine, likely due to the absence of the lateral incisor root, which guides the canine’s eruption path [ 61 ]. Consequently, lateral incisor agenesis has been identified as a potential risk factor for canine displacement and impaction after early SABG. Nevertheless, a systematic review by Lacerda et al. found only low evidence linking canine impaction to lateral incisor agenesis [ 62 ]. Additional predictors for canine impaction in cleft patients include advanced root development, apical tooth position, sharp axial angulation, older age at the time of SABG, and the need for regrafting [ 11 , 63 – 65 ]. In our cohort, impacted canines were successfully managed with surgical exposure and orthodontic traction without adverse effects on graft stability or periodontal health. These results support a risk-benefit approach where early SABG can be performed safely with radiographic monitoring and interdisciplinary planning. Periodontal evaluation and interdisciplinary care Periodontal stability and inflammation-free tissues are crucial for long-term graft success. Previous literature has shown that SABG does not adversely affect the periodontal integrity of teeth adjacent to the cleft, with attachment levels comparable to those of non-cleft teeth [ 66 , 67 ]. In our cohort, probing depths of all cleft-sided teeth remained within the healthy range (< 3 mm) and did not differ significantly from those of the contralateral side in patients with UCLP. Soft tissue outcomes such as papilla presence or gingival inflammation were not systematically recorded and therefore could not be included. Prospective studies are needed to evaluate these additional aspects of periodontal health. Loss of vitality was seldom observed and was not associated with either the surgical intervention or subsequent orthodontic treatment. Neither periodontal health nor vitality was correlated with the timing of SABG, the presence of lateral incisors, or the type of space closure. Poor oral hygiene is a known risk factor for surgical failure due to bone loss [ 68 ]. A 20-year follow-up study by Jabbari et al. demonstrated a positive correlation between the gingival bleeding index and prosthetic restorations in cleft areas, leading to increased bone resorption [ 69 ]. Given the increased susceptibility of cleft patients to periodontitis, thorough oral hygiene instructions, regular professional dental care, and strong interdisciplinary collaboration with restorative dentists and periodontists are crucial to ensure the long-term health and stability of the cleft region and minimize the need for regrafting. Limitations Although this study provides important insight, some limitations should be acknowledged. The retrospective single-center study design may introduce selection bias and limit generalizability. Although strict inclusion criteria and a uniform treatment protocol were applied, the retrospective design inherently limited randomization and prospective control. Furthermore, the radiographic assessment relied exclusively on 2D-imaging procedures, which cannot capture bucco-palatal dimensions or volumetric changes at the grafted site. Although OPTGs were the clinical standard during the treatment period and offered longitudinal comparability, they may under- or overestimate graft volume due to anatomical superimpositions. The absence of CBCT prevented evaluation of 3D graft morphology and spatial bone preservation. Future studies should incorporate CBCT-based volumetric analysis to validate and expand upon these findings, particularly by assessing long-term graft resorption and integration. Recent studies have already demonstrated the potential of CBCT-based volumetric methods to provide standardized assessments of graft outcomes, underscoring the importance of integrating such approaches in future research [ 70 , 71 ]. Although the imbalance between agenesis and non-agenesis cases may limit the power of subgroup comparison, stratified analysis and careful subgroup evaluation were employed to minimize bias in interpretation. One limitation of this study is the inability to control for potential confounding variables such as cleft type (UCLP vs. BCLP), sex, and the presence or absence of lateral incisor agenesis using multivariate or regression analyses. Intra- and inter-examiner reliability of radiographic measurements (axis-angle, limbus height) was not formally assessed, which may limit reproducibility of the findings. Due to the retrospective nature of the dataset and inconsistencies in clinical documentation over time, applying such statistical models without introducing bias was not feasible. Nevertheless, these variables may influence graft outcomes and should be taken into account when interpreting the results. Future prospective studies with standardized protocols and larger sample sizes are essential to multivariable statistical analyses and to clarify the influence of these confounding variables on treatment outcomes.

[conclusion] Conclusions SABG timing is a decisive factor in treatment outcomes in patients with CLP. Using R-value as a developmental reference provides a reliable method for optimizing graft timing. Performing SABG during early canine mineralization stages ( R ≤ 0,5) promotes bone preservation and facilitates orthodontic space closure, especially in patients with lateral incisor agenesis. Furthermore, bone resorption in the graft and adjacent to the limbus alveolaris was significantly lower following orthodontic space closure compared to prosthodontic treatment alternatives. Early SABG, at the R ≤ 0,5 stage, may slightly increase the risk of canine impaction or transposition with the lateral incisor. However, these complications can be effectively managed through surgical canine exposure and orthodontic extrusion without leading to higher bone resorption rates or periodontal damage. While our findings reflect a single-center experience, the alignment of our treatment protocol with those used across European cleft centers supports their broader applicability. In conclusion, this study underscores the crucial role of precisely tailored and optimally timed SABG in optimizing functional and structural outcomes in cleft site management while minimizing potential complications. Future research should integrate CBCT-based volumetric assessment with additional clinical and biomechanical parameters in order to refine treatment protocols and enhance outcomes for patients with CLP.

retrieved_papers.title: Treatment of ectopic eruption of the maxillary first permanent molar in children and adolescents: A scoping review.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2022]
[Abstract] Ectopic eruption of the first permanent molar (EFUPM) is defined as its local disturbed eruptive behaviour, positioning too mesially against the distal aspect of the second upper primary molar during the developing mixed dentition. The EFUPM prevalence is up to 6% and affects both genders equally. The present study aimed to perform a scoping review to collect the most relevant studies published in the last 30 years and focused on the different strategies, traditional and novel, for treating irreversible uni- or bilateral EFUPM in children. This review also aimed to make recommendations and map the gaps in this clinical topic.
An electronic and manual search was conducted in five databases according to previously established eligibility criteria, using different combinations of keywords, MeSH terms, and Boolean operators. Titles, abstracts, and full-text articles were screened and selected by pre-calibrated reviewers. A data charting was also accomplished for summarising the overview of the evidence.
Paediatric dentists must learn to diagnose and treat this condition early in order to allow the prevention of future malocclusions and other clinical sequelae. Practitioners have at their disposal a variety of corrective options available for the successful resolution of EFUPM.

[Introduction] Background Tooth eruption is a complex process involving the migration of the formed tooth from its developmental position within the bone to its functional position on the dental arch. The process may be subject to disruption for a variety of reasons, including genetic, cellular, molecular, or tissue-related factors. In the absence of harmonisation, the forces acting on the developing tooth structure may hinder the achievement of proper occlusion. A variety of issues may arise during the phase of tooth transition. One such example is ectopic eruption (EE) [ 1 – 3 ]. EE is defined as the eruption of a tooth in an abnormal position [ 4 – 6 ]. The abnormal eruption path is known to cause EE of permanent first molars (PFMs), which may result in the tooth becoming impacted under the distal undercut of the primary second molar (PSM) [ 5 , 6 ]. The prevalence of EE of PFMs varies between 0.75% and 6% across different populations [ 7 , 8 ]. It is thought that multifactorial factors, including local and genetic factors, play a role in the aetiology of EE. Extensive studies have identified the following aetiological factors: posterior positioning or a small upper jaw in relation to the skull base, inadequate anterior movement of primary teeth, an abnormal eruption path for permanent molars, differences in tooth and bone size, the growth of the tuber, delayed development and calcification of PFMs, crowding, malocclusion, small arches, familial predisposition, cleft lip and palate, primary teeth and large molars, are among these aetiological factors [ 5 , 9 , 10 ]. EE of the PFMs has been classified in various ways in the literature, based on factors such as the spontaneous correction of its position, its effect on PSMs and its severity [ 11 ]. Accurate diagnosis and timely management of EE are essential to safeguard the health of both the permanent molars and PSMs. In the majority of cases, diagnosis is made using radiographic imaging, typically in the form of panoramic or periapical views [ 5 , 6 , 12 ]. The selection of treatment is contingent upon the severity of the condition in question, as well as the probability of spontaneous resolution. In cases of a mild nature, close monitoring may suffice. However, in more advanced cases, the use of elastic separators, orthodontic appliances, or surgical intervention is often necessary [ 5 , 6 , 12 ]. In the absence of intervention, the consequences of EE can include root resorption, premature tooth loss and a reduction in arch length. These factors have the potential to contribute to malocclusion [ 13 ]. Consequently, the promotion of normal occlusal development and the minimisation of the necessity for future orthodontic treatment are contingent upon the early detection and implementation of appropriate interventions. As EE constitutes a substantial dental anomaly, the execution of population-based prevalence studies is imperative to facilitate the prevention of the such outcomes mentioned above. However, there is a paucity of comprehensive epidemiological data on EE, especially with regard to variations across different age groups and between sexes [ 5 , 6 , 11 – 13 ]. Furthermore, although classification systems based on severity have been proposed, their practical relevance and influence on clinical decision-making are still being debated [ 12 , 13 ]. In this context, the assessment of the frequency of EE in younger age groups provides valuable regional data that contributes to the existing body of research and supports the development of more effective preventive orthodontic strategies. The objective of the current study was to determine the prevalence of EE of PFMs on the panoramic radiographs of children aged between 5 and 8 years. The null hypothesis (H0) was that there would be no statistically significant difference between the sexes of participants with EE and no statistical difference between PFM type and grades of EE.

[methods] Methods Study design and followed checklists The current work has an observational, cross-sectional and retrospective study design. In this retrospective cross-sectional study, the prevalence of EE was determined in panoramic radiographs previously taken from patients for various indications, and related factors were investigated. This observational study also followed the STROBE (Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology) checklist, which was developed to improve the quality of observational cross-sectional studies, as discussed in previous research [ 14 ]. Ethical approval and Informed Consent Forms Ethical committee approval for the research protocol was obtained from the Ankara University Faculty of Dentistry Clinical Research Ethics Committee on 22.04.2024, with the decision number of 06/04. As the research design was a retrospective observational study, patients (or their parents/caregivers) who had previously given verbal and written informed consent for a panoramic radiographic examination were included in the study procedures. Also, the current study was conducted in accordance with the World Medical Association (WMA) Declaration of Helsinki’s ethical principles for medical research involving human participants or identifiable human material or data ( https://www.wma.net/policies-post/wma-declaration-of-helsinki/ ) [ 15 ]. Sample size analysis Assuming that the prevalence of EE was found to be 0.75% − 6% in previous literature [ 8 ], a minimum panoramic radiograph sample of 4037 individuals was found to be sufficient for the study procedures when the sample calculation was performed using the One Sample Proportion test with a power of 0.80 at a significance level of 0.05. Study procedure The study included panoramic radiographs with sufficient image quality for diagnostic evaluation. The inclusion of panoramic radiographs in the study procedures was carried out within the scope of the eligibility criteria listed below. To ensure standardization in terms of image quality, radiographic images obtained using the same radiography device and mode were included in the study procedures. In this context, panoramic radiographs were included which had previously taken with a digital panoramic x-ray device, Planmeca ProOne ® (2016-02), using 62 kVp, 4 mA in pediatric mode. Additionally, digital panoramic radiographs and related patient information were obtained from the patient automation system of the Ankara University, Faculty of Dentistry Hospital (Synapse 7.2.000), filtered for each year and age group respectively. Patient selection and eligibility criteria The eligibility criteria were applied to all the 5051 panoramic radiographs. 4426 radiographs (17704 PFMs) were found eligible for this research. The inclusion criteria for panoramic radiographs in the study protocol to perform the study procedures were as follows (by Ö.B.S. and N.S.Ö.): Inclusion criteria Panoramic radiographs with the following features were included; belonging to patients aged 5–8 years. taken from the patients who were medically free of chronic/systemic conditions. taken from the patients without any dental anomalies other than EE of PFMs. taken from the patients whose PFM teeth had not been extracted for any reason. Exclusion criteria Panoramic radiographs with the following features were excluded; belonging to patients aged < 5 years or > 8 years. taken from the patients who had chronic/systemic conditions. taken from the patients with dental anomalies other than EE of PFMs. taken from the patients whose PFM teeth had been extracted for any reason. Panoramic radiograph assessment and Inter-Examiner reliability Two independent investigators recorded the following for each patient: date of birth; date of panoramic radiography taken; age in years and months; sex; presence or absence of EE; if present, the PFM (right or left; unilateral or bilateral; upper or lower jaw); and the degree of EE. The assessment of the first 550 panoramic radiograph were subjected to inter-examiner reliability (Kappa) test for both investigators and inter-examiner reliability was found to be approximately 0.91, demonstrating good reliability. Based on the good inter-examiner reliability, the remaining panoramic radiographs were assessed by only one investigator (either Ö.B.S. or N.S.Ö). Grading the level of ectopic eruption EE was classified according to resorption in PSMs, as described by Barberia-Leache et al. [ 13 ]. According to this classification: Grade I (mild): Limited resorption of cementum or minimal penetration into dentin. Grade II (moderate): Resorption of dentin without pulp exposure. Grade III (severe): Distal root resorption with pulp exposure. Grade IV (very severe): Resorption affecting the mesial root of PSMs. Statistical analysis The data obtained were analyzed using SPSS v22 software (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA). Categorical variables were summarized as counts (n) and percentages (%), continuous variables as mean ± SD or median (IQR), as appropriate. In addition to frequency and percentage distributions of the data, the relationship between variables was examined by Chi-Square and Binomial tests. All binomial tests in the current study were performed as exact two-sided tests instead of using normal approximation, in order to provide more accurate and conservative p values. The significance level was set at 0.05.

[results] Results A total of 4426 patients (retrospectively scanned panoramic radiographs) and 17,704 teeth of these cases detected on panoramic radiographs were included in the study. Prevalence rates were calculated as case-based (patients with at least one tooth affected by EE) and tooth-based (proportion of all examined teeth affected by EE). Accordingly, the prevalence of case-based EE was 1.51%, while tooth-based prevalence was 0.60% (Table 1 ). Table 1 Case-based and tooth-based prevalence of EE EE Cases Teeth n % n % Present 67 1.51 107 0.60 Absent 4359 98.49 17,597 99.40 Total 4426 100 17,704 100 n : number, % : percentage, EE : Ectopic Eruption. Of the 4426 patients, 2275 (51.40%) were male and 2151 (48.60%) were female. Among 107 teeth affected by EE, 59 (55.14%) belonged to males and 48 (44.86%) to females. On a case basis, 37 cases with EE (55.22%) were male and 30 (44.78%) were female. The sex distribution among EE cases did not deviate from the expected 1:1 ratio (two-sided exact binomial test, p = 0.464) (Table 2 ). Table 2 Sex distribution of patients diagnosed with EE (case-based) Sexes Cases with EE Binomial test (two-sided) n % p Male 37 55.22 0.464 Female 30 44.78 Total 67 100.0 n : number, % : percentage, p : probability value (significance level set at 0.05), EE : Ectopic Eruption The mean age of the patients diagnosed with EE was 6.68 ± 0.76 years (Table 3 ). Also, distribution of the age variable with Kernel Density Estimation was shown in Fig. 1 . Additionally, Fig. 2 showed scatter plot of the age variable. Accordingly, the age of most participants were concentrated in the range of 6.49* to 6.87** (*Lower and **upper bound, 95% confidence interval). This indicated that the distribution of the data set is concentrated within a narrow confidence interval. However, in general, the distribution showed a symmetrical spread and is clustered close to the mean. Table 3 Age distribution in cases with EE EE Age n Mean Median Min Max SD Present 67 6.68 6.67 5.00 7.92 0.76 Absent 4359 6.35 6.25 5.00 7.92 0.86 Total 4426 6.35 6.25 5.00 7.92 0.86 n : number, Min : minimum, Max : maximum, SD : standard deviation, EE : Ectopic Eruption Fig. 1 Distribution of the age variable with Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) Fig. 2 Scatter Plot of the age variable (95% confidence interval) Table 4 showed how much each type of PFM was affected by EE. Accordingly, #16 was the most affected by EE (46.73%), #46 was the least affected (4.67%). Additionally, 96 teeth (89.72%) and 11 teeth (10.28%) showed EE in the upper and lower jaw, respectively. In terms of level of EE (regardless of PFM type) Grade 2 was the most commonly observed (42.99%), while Grade 4 was the least detected (9.35%). However, no statistically significant difference was observed between PFM type and EE grades (Chi-Square test with Monte Carlo simulation, p = 0.318) (Table 4 , Figs. 3 , 4 , 5 and 6 ). This distribution did not deviate from the expected 1:1 ratio (two-sided exact binomial test, p = 0.847) (Table 5 ). Grade 2 level was more frequently observed on the right and left side (40% and 46.15%, respectively). However, no statistically significant difference was observed between the side and the grade level of EE ( p = 0.696) (Table 6 ). Table 6 Statistical comparison between side (right vs. left) and EE grades Grade for EE Side Chi-Square test Right (#16 + #46) Left (#26 + #36) n % n % χ2 p Grade 1 13 23.64 9 17.31 1.44 0.696 Grade 2 22 40.00 24 46.15 Grade 3 16 29.09 13 25.00 Grade 4 4 7.27 6 11.54 Total 55 100.00 52 100.00 n : number, % : percentage, χ2 : chi-square, p : probability value (significance level set at 0.05), EE : Ectopic Eruption Among 107 PFMs diagnosed with EE, 96 (89.72%) were in the upper jaw and 11 (10.28%) in the lower jaw. Also, this distribution deviated markedly from an expected 1:1 ratio (two-sided exact binomial test, p < 0.001) (Table 7 ). The difference in the bilateral/unilateral distribution between the upper and lower jaws was not statistically significant (χ²(1) = 0.47, two-sided with Yates’ correction, p = 0.494) (Table 8 ). This distribution did not differ from an expected 1:1 ratio (two-sided exact binomial test, p = 0.306). In the lower jaw, 3 (37.50%) were bilateral and 5 (62.50%) unilateral, likewise showing no departure from 1:1 ( p = 0.727) (Table 9 ). [discussion] Discussion This study evaluated the prevalence of EE of PFMs in children aged 5–8 years. The results showed that the prevalence of EE (case based) was 1.51%, with the highest prevalence in the upper jaw. Also, according to obtained data, the null hypothesis was accepted. EE of the PFMs refers to an abnormal deviation from its natural eruption pathway [ 11 ]. This condition is classified as a multifactorial pathological disorder that may be influenced by genetic and familial predispositions. Additional aetiological factors include aberrant angulation of PFMS, upper jaw hypoplasia, upper jaw retrognathism, macrodontia and anomalies in the calcification of PFMs [ 11 , 16 , 17 ]. EE is frequently overlooked during clinical and radiographic examinations. Consequently, paediatric dentists must be vigilant and possess the expertise to intervene early to prevent PFMs becoming irreversibly impacted. Failure to address this condition may result in the premature loss of PSMs, which can lead to further orthodontic complications such as loss of occlusal function, aesthetic impairments, pain, infection, resorption of adjacent tissues, crowding and space deficiencies [ 5 , 11 , 17 , 18 ]. Investigating the prevalence of EE in PFMs on a community basis is therefore crucial for optimising individual treatment modalities. In line with this, the aim of this study was to investigate the prevalence of EE in our institution using demographic indicators. A review of the extant literature revealed that the population included in EE studies was of different age ranges, as was summarised below. In this context, although the age ranges are almost similar, it can be posited that there are minor methodological differences in previous studies. In the current study, the intention was to include children within the 5–8 age range within the methodology. This age range corresponds to the early mixed dentition period, when PFMs typically begin to erupt in the oral cavity. The eruption of PFMs represents a critical juncture in the providing of acceptable occlusion. Dental anomalies, such as EE, during this phase can result in substantial complications, including the loss of space within the dental arch, the occurrence of root resorption of PSMs and the manifestation of varying degrees of malocclusion [ 1 – 6 ]. Consequently, the present study incorporated panoramic radiographic images of pediatric patients within the specified age range (5–8 years). In this particular context, it was considered that restricting the sample to this developmental period ensured homogeneity within the study population and strengthened the clinical validity of the obtained findings. In terms of the prevalence of EE of PFMs, Anoush et al. [ 19 ] found a prevalence rate of 2.1% in children aged 5–8 years; Shojaeipour et al. [ 20 ] found a rate of 2.8% in children aged 6–9 years and Salbach et al. [ 21 ] found a rate of 1.3% in children aged 5–9 years. Another study, conducted on a Turkish population of children aged 5–11 years, investigated the prevalence of EE of PFMs [ 8 ]. Güven [ 8 ] found EE in 2.65% of children, while Çalışkan et al. [ 22 ] found it in 0.83% of children aged 6–10 years and Kurkutan et al. [ 5 ] in 2% of children aged 5–12 years. The prevalence of EE of PFMs has been reported to range from 0.75 to 6% [ 8 , 23 ]. The present study’s prevalence results are similar to those reported in the literature, with an EE prevalence of 1.51% (case-based) and 0.60% (tooth-based). By focusing on the 5–8 age range, the study aimed to highlight the importance of early diagnosis and prompt treatment. Additionally, this study found the highest prevalence of EE in children aged 6.68 years. Differences in EE rates may be due to various factors, including age group, number of patients, ethnicity, genetic factors, and caries status of the study population. Furthermore, this study aligns with other studies in the literature that found no sex difference in the presence of EEs [ 7 , 13 , 24 ]. The present study found that the prevalence of EE of PFMs in the upper jaw was significantly higher than in the lower jaw. This finding is consistent with those of studies by Güven et al. [ 8 ], Anoush et al. [ 19 ], Salbach et al. [ 21 ], Çalışkan et al. [ 22 ] and Mooney et al. [ 25 ]. However, it contradicts the findings of Chintakanon and Boonpinon [ 7 ], who showed that EEs were similar in the upper jaw and the lower jaw. Additionally, in a study conducted on permanent second molar, Bondemark et al. [ 24 ] found that the prevalence of EE was higher in the lower jaw than in the upper jaw, which contradicts the results of this study. We hypothesise that the higher prevalence of EE in the upper jaw is due to its posterior position relative to the cranial base and insufficient bone growth in the tuberosity region, which is consistent with previous literature. Additionally, in this study, no statistically significant differences were found between sexes in terms of EE, both on a case and tooth basis analyses. In this regard, it was thought that sex was not a decisive indicator or risk factor in terms of EE, but as authors, we recommend that this finding needs to be confirmed by further clinical studies. Salbach et al. [ 21 ], Bondemark et al. [ 24 ] and Mooney et al. [ 25 ] found no difference between the right and left sides in their studies on EE of PFMs, and the results were similar. The present study corroborates the findings of previous studies. However, contrary to most studies in the literature, including the present study, Barberia-Leache et al. [ 13 ] reported that EE on the right side was more frequent and severe than on the left. These differing results may be due to incidental factors or differences in the parameters of the study design. EE was classified according to resorption in PSMs in the current study. Accordingly, the clinical implications of the grading system (I to IV) should be emphasised. In mild cases (Grade I), the phenomenon is typically limited to cementum or superficial dentin resorption. These cases have been known to resolve spontaneously, provided that careful observation is undertaken. Conversely, moderate to severe cases (Grade II and III, respectively) entail dentin resorption and, in some cases (Grade III), exposure of the pulp. These cases generally require timely intervention to prevent premature tooth loss or infection. In cases of extreme severity, categorised as Grade IV, where resorption extends to the mesial root of the PSMs, there is a higher risk of early tooth loss, space loss on the dental arch, and subsequent malocclusion [ 13 ]. This underscores the necessity for prompt intervention. Therefore, the classification of EE based on the severity of resorption provides clinicians with valuable prognostic information for planning appropriate follow-up or treatment strategies. Accordingly, in the current study, no statistical difference was observed between EE grades in terms of PFM type, nor was any difference detected between sides (right and left) and the grades (I to IV). This result showed that the type of PFMs and the side on which the PFM is located did not significantly affect root resorption in PSMs. However, it is suggested that further cross-sectional studies with larger samples are needed to confirm this results. The current study found that the rate of bilateral EE in the upper jaw was higher than the rate of unilateral EE. Furthermore, the prevalence of bilateral EE was higher than that of unilateral EE, regardless of the upper jaw or lower jaw. In the previous research assessing unilateral or bilateral occurrence of EE, some studies reported bilateral EE was detected more common than unilateral occurrence [ 13 , 18 ], while some studies reported the contrary [ 20 , 26 , 27 ]. Additionally, Salbach et al. [ 21 ] found that the rate of bilateral EE in the upper jaw was higher than the rate of unilateral EE, consistent with the results of the current study. We hypothesise that the high rate of bilateral EE of PFMs, which is consistent with previous studies, may be due to the general structure of the upper jaw. Additionally, this situation may affect other teeth in the upper jaw or upper jaw development. Although the present study had large sample regarding evaluating EE, it had some limitations. Initially, panoramic radiographs were used to detect EE. While panoramic radiographs provide valuable information, they also have limitations in terms of poor diagnostic quality and possible superposition. Second, the present study was conducted in a single centre. While a significant population was screened, multicentre studies are needed to increase the number of patients and examine the prevalence of EE and its relationship with dento-skeletal anomalies by including larger populations and racial variations. Despite its limitations, this study is expected to facilitate future research into EE.

[conclusion] Conclusions This study highlights the importance of radiographic evaluation for detecting EE in children aged 5–8 years. Pediatric dentists should therefore pay particular attention during examinations, as early diagnosis is important for starting treatment at the most appropriate time. Early diagnosis and treatment can be advantageous for patients and parents in terms of saving time and money, and reducing the likelihood of complicated treatment. For this reason, it is extremely important for children to have regular dental check-ups. Future studies may contribute to the establishment of clinical guidelines for diagnosis, treatment and follow-up of prognosis. These findings should be confirmed by further comprehensive clinical observations or trials. Also, further studies that more accurately predict risk indicators for PFMs affected by EE may enable more reliable assessment of cases in this regard. In this context, future studies could investigate the development of AI- and machine learning-based tools, such as decision trees, to aid in the clinical diagnosis or prediction of EE.

retrieved_papers.title: Factors influencing treatment duration of impacted maxillary canines.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To determine factors associated with treatment duration in impacted maxillary canines (IMCs) using orthopantomography and cone beam computed tomography.
Seventeen linear and angular measurements were evaluated using pretreatment radiographic images of 64 patients (83 IMCs). Treatment duration from surgical exposure until the canine appeared in the oral cavity and until achieving ideal alignment were recorded in months.
Average treatment duration until appearance in the mouth was 14.61 ± 9.28 months and 26.98 ± 12.44 months until ideal alignment. Buccally impacted canines emerged in 6.56 ± 3.24 months and were ideally aligned in 19.44 ± 8.49 months in comparison with palatally positioned at 16.34 ± 9.19 and 28.91 ± 12.77 months, respectively. Canines localized around lateral incisors had shorter treatment times than those localized around central incisors. Canines that were inclined >30° to the sagittal medial line (SML) had significantly longer treatment time until initial appearance in the mouth (16.31 ± 9.32) compared with those inclined less (10.82 ± 8.92). Linear regression analyses demonstrated that these factors were predictors for both treatment duration until emergence and ideal alignment of the canine.
Treatment duration for IMCs is lengthy, particularly for palatally and mesially positioned canines and those with greater inclinations. Palatally located canines typically require twice as much time to emerge as those positioned buccally. Canines located nearer the central incisor area take twice as much time to emerge as those located in the lateral incisor area.

[methods] Materials and Methods Seventeen linear and angular measurements were evaluated using pretreatment radiographic images of 64 patients (83 IMCs). Treatment duration from surgical exposure until the canine appeared in the oral cavity and until achieving ideal alignment were recorded in months.

[results] Results Average treatment duration until appearance in the mouth was 14.61 ± 9.28 months and 26.98 ± 12.44 months until ideal alignment. Buccally impacted canines emerged in 6.56 ± 3.24 months and were ideally aligned in 19.44 ± 8.49 months in comparison with palatally positioned at 16.34 ± 9.19 and 28.91 ± 12.77 months, respectively. Canines localized around lateral incisors had shorter treatment times than those localized around central incisors. Canines that were inclined >30° to the sagittal medial line (SML) had significantly longer treatment time until initial appearance in the mouth (16.31 ± 9.32) compared with those inclined less (10.82 ± 8.92). Linear regression analyses demonstrated that these factors were predictors for both treatment duration until emergence and ideal alignment of the canine.

[conclusion] Conclusions Treatment duration for IMCs is lengthy, particularly for palatally and mesially positioned canines and those with greater inclinations. Palatally located canines typically require twice as much time to emerge as those positioned buccally. Canines located nearer the central incisor area take twice as much time to emerge as those located in the lateral incisor area.
